speaker: interviewer
text: October 20, 1996. Survivor, Milton Belfer. B-E-L-F-E-R. And this is tape three. You were telling us about the children working also because they got a cup of soup at lunch time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did the children fare in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody have to work. Children, they have the privilege they went from-- I should say, from six, eight years to work. So because they should get a-- a soup. And it was very tragic. These children goes to work. But they was anxious to get a soup. We have-- the ghetto was very well organized, but the condition of living, food, and-- living condition was not so bad as food. We didn't-- everybody had a shelter. Not like normal have to be, but they could have a shelter. For instance, they put in 10 families in one room. So everyone was behind a roof. It happened like this. We have-- in the ghetto, we have a lot of resource, they call it. Factories, another way in English. Which we produced everything, almost. Everything for the German. We made shoes. We made clothing. We made uniform. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Slippers. All kind, what you could think of it, we produce. But went everything to the Germans. They give the raw material. We give the labor, and went back. And I don't know exactly what [YIDDISH] we had between the Germans and our leaders from the ghetto. But they-- they supply us with food, what was. We get in-- finding how I say it. Sugar. Everything for the-- potatoes, we get in. Every day came [POLISH] with food for the ghetto. And we have-- in the ghetto, we have a department of distribution. For instance, like I said before, I worked in a distribution which I had food. All kind of food, I distribute. And we had around-- I think around 60 stalls, deposits, which we deposit to people on those carts. But we get this from the Germans. We could not-- we Jews, we-- we paid for the food. And we have to deliver this to the-- to the main office.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you paid for the food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get paid for your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then that's the way you were able to pay for the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much. I should say yes. I get paid for the work. Everybody gets paid for the work. And with this, we have to pay for the ration. We had a ration. And this ration supposed to feed two weeks, and a bread for eight days. But was very little. And they have some calculation this-- the-- the money what they got, we got our own money. We didn't have German marks. We had our ghetto money, I should say. And the ghetto money, we paid with the ghetto money. We received the ghetto money. Like what we are now the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the children. How did they manage, with the children? Were they able to stay with their parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The children were staying with their parents. They did not-- but was a few times-- this I-- you remind me. A selection. Let me see. I was living in this building. I had to go down. Everybody from the building has to go down on the street, or I say in the yard of the house, how it is built, the house. And the German looked over everybody. And who he didn't like, he put them in on the-- in the-- I would say in the truck. Put them up on the truck, and the truck was going and coming. They closed up streets, and they went from house to house. Because somebody could not go down on the-- so they went up house by house, see to somebody is not hiding who could hide himself. And they took away the children. They put them in-- in a hospital. There was a hospital. They put the children in. And the trucks-- I had permission to walk in this time. Was-- how do I say-- a-- they closed-- nobody is allowed to go. Only special people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So special people, they advise before, got a document and got a band. Special yellow band. But I was working the [POLISH], and I was the nearest this. So I was permit to see all the [POLISH]. Because everybody have to be home, so I was allowed in this time to go on in the street. So they took the children from their house in a truck. They bring in a hospital. And so was going around the ghetto. Other trucks from the Germans, military, they throw down the children from the windows, from the hospital. There was a hospital. On the truck. They laid the covers. And take them away, what we saw the blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not allowed to go. Especially in this part. Even me, I have to go-- but you see it from far away, one corner away. This, we had it a few times in the ghetto. Before the liquidation, we had it a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your leader, Jewish governor in the ghetto, was he able to prevent them picking up the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He did not-- he was, I should say a puppet. They told him to do. They told him, for instance, was a requisition from the ghetto. So the Germans said-- they had a plan. We had in the ghetto 150,000 Jews or more, because they-- from around the city, from other cities, little towns, they send them to our-- to Lodz. Those little towns. Like my wife is from another town, little town. They send her to Lodz. And I am from Lodz. So they taking out to concentration camp, to Auschwitz, to Bergen. And they brought in other people from the little towns. So they took those-- the-- the children, especially, was once-- was closed for three days, I think. Nobody could go on the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they did it like I said before. They concentrate the Jews. Later came trucks. And they throw down and take them away. And the Jewish population in Lodz, in the ghetto, got lower always because they kill more, they send away, more than it came in from the little towns.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later on, was almost Judenfrei, how they call it. They didn't want to have Jews. Free of Jews. From all around and they concentrated only in Lodz. Like in Warsaw. And all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: When these refugees came in from the surrounding towns, did they bring news of what was happening outside in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know less than we, why. We have-- like we see sometime people have Polacks or even maybe Germans, friends. They throw in a newspaper over, and somebody picked it up. So we have it some communication, somebody had hidden radios. We knew it, in the little-- big city. In small city, it was more danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a resistance movement in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We have a movement which one tell that the news. But not a resistant. We have-- I had friends which had-- I had a guy which he had a radio hidden, and he came to me. He didn't use every day because he was afraid. And he came. He hear something new, he came to tell me. I was a friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans allow these radios?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen if they found one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kill them. There was one famous-- he had a-- a radio and also a transmitter. And they find out-- they had ways to find out. I couldn't tell you exactly. I know one not far from us, I knew him. He was older than me. And they picked him up just when he was listening to radio. They knew, whatever it was. They had-- we don't know exactly, it was announced or not, but it happened. Happened a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent him immediately, take him out of the ghetto, kill him. They didn't make a big-- and the Jewish leadership didn't have any power to stop them. He didn't give over to the Jews, a-- a person like this. They take care of themselves. They put them in the criminal polizei. The criminal polizei took them away immediately from the ghetto. Because we had a lot of Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Germans for the, how you say criminal police. And we have a little Jews which are-- was denouncing. We knew it. But we couldn't-- they went in there. They have some news. They tell them news. Spies, I should say. We have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that there was a hospital. Was there medicine, doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a hospital, doctors. But we-- we make it every time small. I mean, the Germans make it smaller. We had till the last minute. We had a big hospital-- a big hospital, which this hospital was donated by the rich-- rich Jewish family, the most of it. And was time by time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was smaller, the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews picked up for forced labor? Were they taken out of the ghetto to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I personally no. They picked up people, which by the-- we had-- they did it different. The-- the Jewish-- how do I say-- community leadership, how many people they need for labor to come back the same day, the Jewish give them. But every day was another announcement which the German alone took people out. They send them away to forced labor like my brother was. You should register. And every thought this-- everybody thought this will be better than the ghetto. Or maybe you could run away, or what. So they went on forced labor, which was worse than the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shulom, the next from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did he manage then, with this labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back. They-- this was in a city, Poznan. They work on the-- work in making-- how we say-- train connections, the rails. The rails. And I have friends what are still alive which worked with him together, and they didn't get food. And working, you have to work 28 hour day. Not-- when it got dark, they worked also. And very little food. People, young people, they got sick. So what they do, they send the sick back to ghetto. This-- the Jews should feed them. They didn't kill them. But from ghetto, we have a-- a quantity, so many people to send to Auschwitz to crematorium. So those people were the-- the first to go Auschwitz to the crematorium. Because we know now. The time, I didn't know it. This-- all the-- where were Jews, they had to give so many of own people, they said for labor. But we know, they send them to Auschwitz, the crematorium should not stop. And this was for the whole time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how was your brother? How did-- was he taken for labor? Did he go to labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was taken for labor, but he came back. And we know it they going to send him to Auschwitz. And we have good connection ghetto. And we took him out. Connection. We took him out, not legal. Somebody came out with him, only going to bring him back for a minute. This, my mother should see him. So when we was outside, we took him home. Next day, the police came look for him. So-- but he was not sleeping in our house. We don't know nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he lived over, and was quiet. So he lived over. He didn't have a card to get food. Live without a card. So he was staying in our house. And-- but he came back sick, without-- What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shulom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able take-- to take care of him when he was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We did the most of it. We had a little more, I should say, than simple people. We sold. I'm from a rich family. Always we sold something. If it money, you could buy. You could buy. People got-- let me see a bread. So they sell the half. They should buy something else, what they need. And-- and who could afford, they bought this. Was some time. Once in a while, was butter or oil distributions. But they don't need the butter. They sell better the butter. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were saying that you were taking care of your brother, who was sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was my mother and me, sister was already, she living with us. The sister with two children, they went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did Shulom-- did he get better?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We tried everything. We try to help with medicine, outside the ghetto. To But the-- he came back. We new it already, to see he's sick. We went to a doctor. This-- the lungs. And with the lungs, and he didn't survive. He died in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to give him a burial there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a Jewish burial. I know where he is. He has his tomb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any contact that you or your family had with the non-Jews outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: After it was closed? No. No. We know-- I was working in this time in ghetto in a place which I have good contact with people which they tell me the news. Let me see. It was lines. And those people give me a favor. They tell me what-- so I let them in without the line, he should get this, they shouldn't need to stay at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that the Americans and British had landed in France on D-Day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. In D-Day, we was not already in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '40-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '44. In beginning of August. In August. August '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hadn't heard about the-- the D-Day.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know once was, but this was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and was it-- it was some-- but I don't recall it this-- but he was-- I know something happened, but I think this was in '42, '43. '43, in the beginning. I-- when liberation was-- '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any advance warning that the ghetto was going to be liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We knew it. We going to be liquidated. We knew it. So we was sitting a little time. The war finished in-- in May '45. I left the ghetto in '44, in September. Was it, so it was short time. But we knew it they liquidate the ghetto. For this, we had-- for this, I hide it. I, the mother, one brother. The rest was already dead, everything. They-- they caught us, like I repeat before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hiding in a cellar. Let me see, in a room. Was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was a little house, but the house was inside, out away of the street. And we put some garbage before this. And this house was a cellar. In the small towns, maybe you know it, was this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we make the cellar bigger. And we put up the tops. And we was laid there. Was a lot of people there. There was a lot of people hiding. I have here friends also. And we-- somebody from the people which was with us went out in the night. He said a-- he saw a lot of Polacks. So it's not-- the-- in the ghetto, they couldn't come in. And this belonged to the ghetto. Not on the Polack side. So we thought this is end the war. We didn't have good communication. So we went out, a few people only went out. My brother, two people. And his brother-in law. Went out and came soldiers passed by, German soldiers passed by with a Jeep. And they caught them. Where's the family? They knew it already. So they took us out, and they-- they took us to the train station. We went to Auschwitz. Who was with you when you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. This brother with his wife. This brother had a child. From my family, it was only this brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel. Which he lived over the war. And my sister with the children has already gone with the husband to Auschwitz. That was a-- a selection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took it-- the Germans took out the whole family. They went-- everybody have to go down on the-- from the building on the main floor, I should say. So-- and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when the Germans came to get your family out of the hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was yelling. This, we didn't had any feelings. The Germans. Them-- and they-- they shot-- they didn't shot us. In the top, and there was yelling and yelling. And immediately they put us up in a-- came-- immediately came a street car. And he shot out a few times, came more Germans with Jeeps. Their car, their types. Not American Jeep. Their types. And they put us in the street car, and all around the soldiers, and we went. And this-- with us in this car was a Dr. Weiskopf. I mention, which is-- is very famous. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, I know him because I worked with his sister-in-law together. Dr. Weis-- said to me, you know you're going to Auschwitz. So I said, what can I do? So he said, if we gone a curve, he going to disappear. I should go with him. I had my mother and the whole family. And when we get a curve on this street, he-- the Germans saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ran off after him, but the train continue. So he hide himself. But the German find out where. They killed him. He was one of the very known. Dr. Weiskopf, in Lodz ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the train. From the train, immediately to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a train was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freight. Not-- it was not second class. It was freight. Freight train. A freight car. We were laying on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. What I had. Yes. What we had prepared a-- I would say a rucksack. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A bag, I would say. We were prepared a bag, and came to Auschwitz. You have to leave everything and go out and separate men, woman, children immediately. And there was the-- there was this soldiers and those couples, probably you heard what's mean couple. And you couldn't do nothing. You went to the-- but the-- thank God I was lucky. My brother helped me a lot. He was older than me. Helped me a-- a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the train ride. How long was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took overnight. Overnight. But he was staying and going. They make it special, we should come early in morning. So-- and start the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was said. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You know what means this? [GERMAN] means work. [GERMAN] is your life. Was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the truth about Auschwitz at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it, yes. Everybody knew it. But you had the chain on the-- you couldn't do nothing. People were throwing away what-- everybody tried to save. But we was one-- how we say-- pull the other. We didn't have the other chances. And Lodz was a city which was around living people there. You-- was not woods, you could run away to the woods. Was a city. People in the small towns ran to the woods, which was big woods. You could go on of hundreds of mile in the woods. Lodz was not this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother with you when you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Immediately it was-- it was rushing the-- the people immediately. Woman separately. And there was couples, couples is-- also of Jew, Polacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they make-- how do I say-- so noisy. This-- you couldn't concentrate. You want the separate woman. Children separately, the woman separately, and the men separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to say goodbye to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a little chance. We got-- when we got out of the train, we got a bag of sugar. Everybody got a bag. So she took out a ring-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We will pause now to change the tape.
